text:
Nice <@UEQ6M68H0> I appreciate the feedback! I definitely could have gone into much more detail about each tool / the history of them. If I were braver and had more time I’d probably write something even longer form exploring the journey in the terms you described “progression towards greater information” 

Re: APIs — I agree there ought to be a better way to share functionality between programmers, but the economics of breaking services up across multiple companies does make a lot of sense. It’s less clear to me how you could build something as immensely valuable to developers as Stripe is if their main delivery method was shipping an executable. APIs as they stand today aren’t technically elegant, but the decentralization allows companies to employ value based pricing and fund the development of awesome software. 

Totally with you on version control though :+1:. 

Thanks again for the feedback. Have any good books or papers you’d recommend?

A “world of interchangeable parts” is only possible with standardized interfaces between the parts (hence the ability to interchange). Not sure how to get there. My current belief (which I realized watching the Google demo of the computer calling a human on the phone to make a restaurant reservation) is that these “standardized” interfaces are not actually going to be standardized—our computers are just going to be smarter about making sense of unknown APIs. Automatic adapters or something like that. In the worst case, you’ll have one computer talking to another computer _over the phone using audio_. It’s a flexible interface.

There’s definitely more nuance here than I realized. Thanks for pointing me in the right direction. I’ll figure out a better way of explaining it in the next edition. 

Haha now that I’m reading it as vulnerabilities are what software is built on I can’t unread it! Will rejigger it a bit.

Audio is a flexible interface, but can be quite ambiguous. In order to support Siri, Apple had to inject quite a bit of knowledge about the world, and in a recent talk by Stephen Wolfram, who arguably has the best track record in history for interchangeable parts, he talked about how when he started Wolfram Alpha only had a few heuristics, and now the thing is mostly heuristics. Unlike most of the other prior work, the Wolfram language has not changed hardly at all in 25 years. He's either a very stubborn person, or he did a superb job in the beginning, or perhaps a mixture of both.

He's either a very stubborn person, or he did a superb job in the beginning, or perhaps a mixture of both.

If we had a language with interchangeable parts, then Stripe could give you a code module that presented and validated a credit card form, with all the best practices, and saving tens of thousands of programmers from having to roll their own. Merchant gateways from credit cards are pretty trivial, but interestingly many people store the credit card data which they are not supposed to do at all. Stripe is an example where if you had modular code it would help a lot. In the case of Google Maps however, the magic is all on the server side, and in that situation you will have a lot of API's. But you could reduce the API's by a huge factor if you hypothesize a better connection between the two computers than merely JSON. I find the lowest common denominator approach of JSON strings as the interchange between computers to be very crude. Surely we can do a lot better.

I would have gone with chronological order...
I would have gone with chronological order...
"the Trace" is a funny name for source maps and debugging symbols. Where did that come from?
"the Trace" is a funny name for source maps and debugging symbols. Where did that come from?

It's not so clear what "the Signature" is saying. Is it talking about type systems and compiler error messages? Or is it talking about the later rise of IDE features - autocomplete, red-squiggly-underlined errors, jump to definition? And again "the Signature" is a strange name.

It's not so clear what "the Signature" is saying. Is it talking about type systems and compiler error messages? Or is it talking about the later rise of IDE features - autocomplete, red-squiggly-underlined errors, jump to definition? And again "the Signature" is a strange name.

"the Package, the Version, the Manifest, the Registry" - skips a key factor, the internet. "if you were missing a peer dependency…you may have to order it in the mail" is not a problem that a package manager can solve. Unless all the packages come on CD-ROM... but the internet was widely available around the same time, and weren't BBSs around before CDs?

"the Package, the Version, the Manifest, the Registry" - skips a key factor, the internet. "if you were missing a peer dependency…you may have to order it in the mail" is not a problem that a package manager can solve. Unless all the packages come on CD-ROM... but the internet was widely available around the same time, and weren't BBSs around before CDs?

"the Commit" - what about CVS? SVN? Even SourceSafe? They conceptualized version control differently but they did most of what people needed. Versioning was the really important thing, not git-style commits. Git was invented and designed for a huge software project (Linux) and it was only created because the BitKeeper source control system that Linux previously relied on was no longer free to access. Many people ignored Git until the rise of GitHub, and I ignored Git until a good GUI was available for it (Git Extensions).

Skimming the rest... "the Ticket" is almost entirely devoid of historical information; "the Build" seems to be referring to continuous integration, not builds - I'd say the most important part of CI is not not building, but automated testing and perhaps deployment; you should always check that the software builds before you commit. "the Vulnerability" is not a "primitive".
